pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

CASE STUDY
AVERAGE JOE PROGRAM DESIGN
Note: this is a copy of the anonymized client intake form from one of my clients with comments in
red and my client evaluation at the end.

The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you. If you have
any questions with regards to this form, please do not hesitate to contact me. You
can use whichever measurement system (e.g. metric, imperial) you prefer to fill in
this form. Just state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

 Full legal name: Ryan
 Age: 23
 Height: 176.5cm
 Weight: 154lbs or 70kg
 Body fat percentage (plus measurement technique): ~12%
 Years of training experience: Too long! ~5 years.
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in weight times
reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
 Bench press: DB bench: 30kg each x6 .. Barbell doesn't agree with my
shoulders. Best has been up to 36kg x6 (you can tell I have
regressed a bit throughout this document)
 Squat (excluding body weight): N/A never squat. See below. Lower
back/hips don't like it, and my personal distaste for these made the
gym 'un-fun'.
 Chin-up (excluding body weight): Curren +20kg x6. My best was when I
was 72kg and could do +32.5 x6 (I haven't trained these for ageS)
 Overhead press: N/A Problems OHP'ing in the past. My best for
example back when I was 19 was 75kg x4 at a bodyweight of 76kg

I recommend a healthy dose of skepticism when looking at clients' past personal
records. People have a very strong mnemonic selection bias that romanticizes all
memories ('back in the good old days…'). A 4 rep bodyweight overhead press at age 19
is highly elite and far beyond his general strength level (or genetic indicators as we'll
see below).

What are your fitness goals?
headings:  Years of training experience: Too long! ~5 years.
 Bench press: DB bench: 30kg each x6 .. Barbell doesn’t agree with my
shoulders. Best has been up to 36kg x6 (you can tell I have
regressed a bit throughout this document)
 Squat (excluding body weight):  N/A never squat. See below.  Lower
back/hips don’t like it, and my personal distaste for these made the
 Chin-up (excluding body weight): Curren +20kg x6. My best was when I

 Overhead press: N/A Problems OHP’ing in the past. My best for
example back when I was 19 was 75kg x4 at a bodyweight of 76kg

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Gain upper body mass and be happy with a physique that carries some
decent muscle. My priorities lie with upper body (I know this is 'bro').
Long term I'd like to maintain 10% or below. Admittedly, I hate training
legs, both in terms of the fact that I don't want mine big (they've been big
before, thus, purposely de-trained) hence I don't train them literally at all
(I know this goes against most fitness logic)

Deadlifts (hurt my back before a few yrs ago and my gym doesn't allow
them)

Squats (my hips hate them Additionally I don't enjoy the exercise
At all thus I've never really bothered to learn them, in terms of cost v
benefit.)

^again this is "blasphemy" to majority of gym goers, I know.

Overhead pressing: (inclusive of this is BB bench - both of these I get
mixed responses from shoulders... DB never seems to give me problems
though? As long as I warm up)

How often in a week would you be prepared to train? Note that no session should last
longer than an hour; 2 sessions is maintenance, 3 is doable and twice daily would
be the absolute maximum at which more is no longer better. 4-6 sessions

6 sessions it is then.

What is your occupation? You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal
trainer/manual labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm,
stress and activity level.
I am a management consultant at a global firm.
I don't own a car, so I do need to walk a fair bit, take public transport etc.
Probably average 10-13k steps per day. Walking to the gym is 15min one
way, as is the supermarket etc.

In terms of circadian rhythm, I need to train in the mornings @ 5am. I
always know that I start work at 8.30 am, yet I can never be sure when I
will finish. Often I do need to travel.

Stress can be quite high at times, tough deadlines and lots of delegations.

I realize the importance of Circadian rhythms and aim to be in bed by
9.30 with computer time limited after 8.30pm.

Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies, illnesses, syndromes,
disorders etc.) you have or had:

N/A?

Do you plan to use any PEDs while under my supervision? If so, please list them:
No.

Note that if you answer 'NO' to any of the questions below, I will not be able to take
you on as a client.
 I declare that, while Menno Henselmans is my personal trainer, all the
substances I use are obtained and used in accordance with their governing
laws: YES
 I forego all rights to hold Menno Henselmans liable for any damages caused in
relation to following his advice as my trainer: YES

At this point, you may choose to return this form to me to obtain a price
quote before filling in the rest. Please return the filled in form to
mennohenselmans@bayesianbodybuilding.com and attach at least two full-body
pictures of yourself in clear lighting, preferably including a back double biceps and
front relaxed pose.

Activity level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
 Sedentary (e.g. desk job)
 Somewhat active (e.g. doing groceries or walking your dog but
otherwise sedentary)
 Active (e.g. on your feet much of the day)
 Very active (e.g. involved in manual labor)

Based on his description of his commuting time above, 'Active' would definitely be
more applicable. This is a rare case of a client underreporting his activity level.

Stress level: please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
 Stress-free (e.g. on holiday)
 Only occasional/mild stress (e.g. student not during exam period)
 Average stress (e.g. full-time work with deadlines and commuting)
 High stress (e.g. very high-paced work environment with great
responsibility)
^In between the two of these. (Understand my health is more important
and this not what I want to do for long, stress is a very important stress
marker, so I am aiming to get a job where I can train in afternoons, less
stress, literally no travel etc)

 Wrist circumference: 16.1cm - 6.3"

 How much did you weigh before you started lifting and what was your height
at that point? I was fat until 16 ~77kg, no muscle though. Shorter than
I am now. Then I became super skinny down to 140lbs eating
nothing as a 17yr old. Since then, I have been as heavy 76kg again,
but probably 15-16% body fat.

 Please attach a picture to this form in which the back of your hand is clearly
visible (see example image below).

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Please list all the supplements you are currently taking:
Protein powder & BCAA's
6g fish Oil
1000mcg molybdenum
30mg manganese chelate
50mg zinc picolinate (3x/week)
2000mg Vit-c
500mg N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine
100mcg Vit K-2
5000IU Vit-D
200 mcg selenium
1000mg taurine
2500mg magnesium malate

^Above seems like a lot, but it costs me $1/day at the price I bought it at.
Since taking, I have seen marked improvements in sleep and libido -
which were the primary reasons.

Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail:
Aim to follow biorhythm diet principles (semi-IF)
Pre workout @ 5am: 10g BCAA

Intra workout: 10g BCAA

'Breakfast' @ 7.30am: 35g protein from whey/casein shake +6g fish oil

Lunch @ 1pm: 350g worth of vegetables (cauliflower, carrot, broc)/200g
worth of grassfed beef or pork/2 hardboiled eggs/10g olive oil

Dinner workout day: 600g potato/1 zucchini/100g broccoli/1 onion/garlic
- cooked in 10g butter with either 200-250g beef or chicken and some
tomato sauce at times.

Dinner off day: replace potato with 300g pumpkin (and no 'breakfast, I
incorporate some more fat by way of olive oil at lunch too.)

Before bed: 300g chobani 0% yogurt, 50-100g frozen blueberries, 50-
100g frozen raspberries, 30g organic puffed rice.

Basically maintenance for me is around 2550 calories. Which is
somewhat high for my bodyweight.

I'm not sure whether to diet or to gain… hence, wheel spinning. Thus my
workout days are something like
220p 60-65f 300c …2700cal

I don't do cardio, apart from walking: and off day is ~ 220p/85f/175 ..
2400cal.

Aiming to not do wheat/gluten,

Very occasionally I have work dinners and go out with friends etc.
Generally I try to stick to steak and potato (chips, really) or a burger, as
they're somewhat easy to estimate. If I travel for work (which I hate, and
haven't had to do too much - I generally end up eating sandwiches a lot)

Once a week, on weekends I do generally train with a good buddy of
mine. He's got an awesome physique, and does intermittent fasting/has a
big kcal intake. We generally have some fun here. I stick to my calories
(or go to 3000cal), but it can be sugary stuff/wheat based.

Please describe or attach your current training program in detail:
4 x per week.

Upper (sat or Sunday)
DB Bench: 3-4 x5-7
Pulldown: 3-4 x5-7
Incline DB Bench: 2-3 x7-9
Incline bench braced DB Row: 2-3 x7-9
DB Side laterals: 3-4 x8-10

Lower(if you could call it that) and arms (Monday)
Leg ext: 3x6-8
Incline DB Curls: 3 x8-10
1 Leg rdl: 3 x6-8
Tricep pushdown: 3x8-10
Pallof press: 3x8-10

45 degree hypers superset with leg raise : 3x10

Chest/back (wed)
Db incline bench: 3 x10-12
Incline DB Bench: 3 x10-12
DB Bench: 2 x12-15
Pulldown: 2x12-15
Shrugs: 3x12-15
**Optional cable fly and straight arm pulldown 1 set myo-reps x20 +5's

Shoulders/arms (thurs or fri)
Incline DB curls: 3x10-12
DB OH tricep extension: 3x10-12
DB Laterals: 4 x12-15
Hammer Curls: myo reps x12-15 +4's
Tricep Pushdown: myo reps x12-15 +4's
Reverse peck deck machine (rear delts) 4 x12-15

have to be honest. And my arse and quads grows at an alarming rate
(trained them hard back in 2010) .. it made me uncomfortable and I don't
actually like the look on myself.

N/A

pages.text:
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Client assessment
Ryan's profile is that of an intermediate lifter with below average upper body genetic
potential (weak joints, small frame) and at least average lower body genetic potential (much
larger frame size). His size would classify him as a novice, but based on his training age,
genetic indicators, alleged past strength level and a program that isn't completely terrible, it's
probably unfair to say he's still his size simply because his programs sucked.

Before determining Ryan's basic program parameters, it is necessary to define his goal. Since
Ryan just wants to look good in everyday life. His stress level allows neither a high calorie
bulk nor a low calorie cut, so I've put him on a 5% deficit with a conservative estimate of
15% for his body fat.

Frequency
His anabolic window is probably down to 24 h at most, but with his stress level an genetics,
it's highly unlikely he will tolerate training each body part daily. So I've started him on an
every other day training frequency.

Intensity
As an intermediate, his average training intensity is around 80% for his primary exercises and
65% for his secondary exercises.

Volume
As an intermediate lifter, 18 sets per week would be a good starting point. We've already
reduced his frequency, so if everything else is in check (i.e. sleep, diet, mindfulness therapy
for stress), he should be able to tolerate this, especially since he won't be doing heavy leg
work. Still, the first thing I'd probably do if his progress is subpar is decrease the volume.

No leg work?!
As for the leg work, it's good to discuss with any client that they'll probably regret not
training their legs later on, as most people do when they get more serious with fitness and

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

'chicken leg syndrome' starts becoming noticeable and they develop the acquired taste of
heavy squatting, but as a coach I do not prescribe my clients their goals. I consider myself a
consultant: they are the decision makers. Your client-coach relation is something you will
have to decide for yourself based on your clientele and abilities.

Here's how his training program looked. It's a simple A-B split that's rotated through.

Session A
1a) Dumbbell overhead press 3x80%
1b) Chin-ups 3x80%
2) Wide pull-down 3x70%
3a) Cable lateral raise 3x60% (shoulder issues)
3b) Bayesian curl 2x65%
4) Exercise bike sprints 3x15s - These serve as a nice finisher to the workout while sneaking
in some leg work.

Session B
1) Dumbbell bench press 3x80%
2a) Bayesian fly 3x70%
2b) Shoulder-pulls 3x65%
2c) Cable unilateral overhead triceps extension 3x60%
3) Side planks 3x30s - Without many of the common heavy barbell compound exercises,
some core work is recommended for injury prevention and even hypertrophy.
4) Farmer's walk 3x30s - These serve as grip, trap and core work while again sneaking in
some leg work and being a nice finisher for the training session.

BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Diet
As a bonus, here's the diet I designed for Ryan. Note how I kept the macro ratios very
simple to aid with meal planning in his hectic schedule. Later on we implemented more
circadian rhythm nutrient timing.

Training (6x)

full_text:
--- Page 1 ---
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

CASE STUDY
AVERAGE JOE PROGRAM DESIGN
Note: this is a copy of the anonymized client intake form from one of my clients with comments in
red and my client evaluation at the end.

The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you. If you have
any questions with regards to this form, please do not hesitate to contact me. You
can use whichever measurement system (e.g. metric, imperial) you prefer to fill in
this form. Just state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

 Full legal name: Ryan
 Age: 23
 Height: 176.5cm
 Weight: 154lbs or 70kg
 Body fat percentage (plus measurement technique): ~12%
 Years of training experience: Too long! ~5 years.
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in weight times
reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
 Bench press: DB bench: 30kg each x6 .. Barbell doesn't agree with my
shoulders. Best has been up to 36kg x6 (you can tell I have
regressed a bit throughout this document)
 Squat (excluding body weight): N/A never squat. See below. Lower
back/hips don't like it, and my personal distaste for these made the
gym 'un-fun'.
 Chin-up (excluding body weight): Curren +20kg x6. My best was when I
was 72kg and could do +32.5 x6 (I haven't trained these for ageS)
 Overhead press: N/A Problems OHP'ing in the past. My best for
example back when I was 19 was 75kg x4 at a bodyweight of 76kg

I recommend a healthy dose of skepticism when looking at clients' past personal
records. People have a very strong mnemonic selection bias that romanticizes all
memories ('back in the good old days…'). A 4 rep bodyweight overhead press at age 19
is highly elite and far beyond his general strength level (or genetic indicators as we'll
see below).

What are your fitness goals?


--- Page 2 ---
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

Gain upper body mass and be happy with a physique that carries some
decent muscle. My priorities lie with upper body (I know this is 'bro').
Long term I'd like to maintain 10% or below. Admittedly, I hate training
legs, both in terms of the fact that I don't want mine big (they've been big
before, thus, purposely de-trained) hence I don't train them literally at all
(I know this goes against most fitness logic)

Deadlifts (hurt my back before a few yrs ago and my gym doesn't allow
them)

Squats (my hips hate them Additionally I don't enjoy the exercise
At all thus I've never really bothered to learn them, in terms of cost v
benefit.)

^again this is "blasphemy" to majority of gym goers, I know.

Overhead pressing: (inclusive of this is BB bench - both of these I get
mixed responses from shoulders... DB never seems to give me problems
though? As long as I warm up)

How often in a week would you be prepared to train? Note that no session should last
longer than an hour; 2 sessions is maintenance, 3 is doable and twice daily would
be the absolute maximum at which more is no longer better. 4-6 sessions

6 sessions it is then.

What is your occupation? You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal
trainer/manual labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm,
stress and activity level.
I am a management consultant at a global firm.
I don't own a car, so I do need to walk a fair bit, take public transport etc.
Probably average 10-13k steps per day. Walking to the gym is 15min one
way, as is the supermarket etc.

In terms of circadian rhythm, I need to train in the mornings @ 5am. I
always know that I start work at 8.30 am, yet I can never be sure when I
will finish. Often I do need to travel.

Stress can be quite high at times, tough deadlines and lots of delegations.

I realize the importance of Circadian rhythms and aim to be in bed by
9.30 with computer time limited after 8.30pm.

Client assessment
Ryan's profile is that of an intermediate lifter with below average upper body genetic
potential (weak joints, small frame) and at least average lower body genetic potential (much
larger frame size). His size would classify him as a novice, but based on his training age,
genetic indicators, alleged past strength level and a program that isn't completely terrible, it's
probably unfair to say he's still his size simply because his programs sucked.

Before determining Ryan's basic program parameters, it is necessary to define his goal. Since
Ryan just wants to look good in everyday life. His stress level allows neither a high calorie
bulk nor a low calorie cut, so I've put him on a 5% deficit with a conservative estimate of
15% for his body fat.

Frequency
His anabolic window is probably down to 24 h at most, but with his stress level an genetics,
it's highly unlikely he will tolerate training each body part daily. So I've started him on an
every other day training frequency.

Intensity
As an intermediate, his average training intensity is around 80% for his primary exercises and
65% for his secondary exercises.

Volume
As an intermediate lifter, 18 sets per week would be a good starting point. We've already
reduced his frequency, so if everything else is in check (i.e. sleep, diet, mindfulness therapy
for stress), he should be able to tolerate this, especially since he won't be doing heavy leg
work. Still, the first thing I'd probably do if his progress is subpar is decrease the volume.

No leg work?!
As for the leg work, it's good to discuss with any client that they'll probably regret not
training their legs later on, as most people do when they get more serious with fitness and


--- Page 12 ---
BAYESIAN BODYBUILDING 2014

'chicken leg syndrome' starts becoming noticeable and they develop the acquired taste of
heavy squatting, but as a coach I do not prescribe my clients their goals. I consider myself a
consultant: they are the decision makers. Your client-coach relation is something you will
have to decide for yourself based on your clientele and abilities.

Here's how his training program looked. It's a simple A-B split that's rotated through.

Session A
1a) Dumbbell overhead press 3x80%
1b) Chin-ups 3x80%
2) Wide pull-down 3x70%
3a) Cable lateral raise 3x60% (shoulder issues)
3b) Bayesian curl 2x65%
4) Exercise bike sprints 3x15s - These serve as a nice finisher to the workout while sneaking
in some leg work.

Session B
1) Dumbbell bench press 3x80%
2a) Bayesian fly 3x70%
2b) Shoulder-pulls 3x65%
2c) Cable unilateral overhead triceps extension 3x60%
3) Side planks 3x30s - Without many of the common heavy barbell compound exercises,
some core work is recommended for injury prevention and even hypertrophy.
4) Farmer's walk 3x30s - These serve as grip, trap and core work while again sneaking in
some leg work and being a nice finisher for the training session.
